content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

July 14, 2016

Ariel Pierre Calonne
Santa Barbara City Attorney
P.O. Box 1990
Santa Barbara, CA 93102

Re:    Your Request for Advice
       Our File No. A-16-089

Dear Mr. Calonne:

     This letter responds to your request for advice regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹

     Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

     May Councilmember Rowse participate in a governmental decision involving a proposed ordinance that would allow mobile food vending in Santa Barbara in certain places and at certain times when he owns a business and leasehold in an area affected by the ordinance?

CONCLUSION

     Yes, Councilmember Rowse may participate in the governmental decision involving the proposed ordinance because there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of his financial interests.

FACTS

     You are the City Attorney for the City of Santa Barbara. You are seeking advice on behalf of San Barbara City Councilmember Randy Rowse. The City of Santa Barbara is considering updating its current regulations governing the use of city streets and rights of way for mobile vending of food and other commodities from trucks. Councilmember Rowse owns a restaurant

_______________
¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-16-089
Page No. 2

located on property he leases on Anacapa Street in Santa Barbara. Councilmember Rowse' financial
stakes in the business and the leasehold each exceed $2,000 in value.

The restaurant is known as the Paradise Café. The restaurant website
(http://www.paradisecafe.com) describes the restaurant as follows:

"The Paradise Café is located in a 1915 vintage brick and stucco building that
once housed an Italian bakery. Attached to the main building is a Victorian
structure that served as the proprietor's residence. The La Paloma Café opened at
this location in 1938; serving Mexican food to downtown residents as well as the
servicemen who were based at what is now U.C.S.B. during the Second World
War. The landmark building was leased by the Paradise Café partners in 1981 and
continues to be operated by the remaining original partner, Randy Rowse.

The old bakery building now serves as the Paradise bar and maintains the art-deco
feel of the 30's. The mural facing the patio depicts old time movie star and Santa
Barbara Fiesta favorite Leo Carrillo on horseback. This was said to be one of
Carrillo's favorite haunts after the Fiesta Parade, and he was known to tie up his
horse in front of the cafe during his annual visit.

The Paradise Cafe was the first and only grill to use Santa Maria live oak wood in
the city of Santa Barbara. We grill prime steaks, fresh fish and, of course our
signature Paradise Burger. Our philosophy is simple and straightforward, and that
is to take the highest quality and freshest ingredients possible and let them speak
for themselves. Our staff takes great pride in the continuity of our traditions of
great food, friendly service and patron loyalty."

The draft ordinance would generally relax the City's existing (and unenforceable)
prohibition on any street peddling. The changes to the City's ordinance regarding mobile street
vending are required in order to comply with the California Court of Appeal ruling in Barajas v.
City of Anaheim (1993) 15 Cal.App.4th 1809. In that case, the court determined that California cities
cannot impose a blanket ban that prohibits vending from vehicles on all city streets. However, cities
are able to enact ordinances that provide specific time, place, and manner restrictions on street
vending due to public safety concerns.

The draft ordinance includes several provisions that may raise concerns in the context of
Councilmember Rowse's restaurant business and leasehold. Two provisions require specific
attention. First, the draft ordinance establishes a generally applicable 60-minute limit on the time
that mobile vending trucks can remain at a given location:

"Except as provided in this section, no vendor shall stand, stop or park any
vehicle or any non-motorized wheeled container, such as a wagon or pushcart,
from which merchandise, food, or other items are sold or displayed or offered for
sale, barter or exchange, on any portion of any street, alley, sidewalk or public
property within the city for a period of time that exceeds sixty minutes at any one
location; provided however, that such vehicle or any non-motorized wheeled

File No. A-16-089
Page No. 3

container may not stand or park at all in a place described in subsection (B)(3) of
this section."

Second, the draft ordinance would include an even greater restriction on mobile vending
trucks in a specified city location, dubbed the "Mobile Vendor Parking Restricted Area." This area
is highly congested and, in the opinion of City staff, the public health and safety require more
stringent regulations. Council Member Rowse's business is located within this area. The regulations
provide that:

"No person shall stand, park or operate any vehicle or any non-motorized wheeled
container within or from the public right of way at the following locations, except
from 7:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m.; those streets bounded by and including: Castillo
Street, Michaeltorena Street, Garden Street, and Cabrillo Boulevard; and on
Milpitas Street from the intersection of Milpitas and Quinientos to the intersection
of Milpas and Canon Perdido."

The net effect of these regulations will be to manage mobile food vending in Santa Barbara.
There is no evidence that the regulations would increase or decrease the amount of competition in
the restaurant business for Councilmember Rowse as Councilmember Rowse's restaurant attracts
different clientele from mobile food vendors.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits public officials from participating in governmental decisions in
which they have a financial interest. Section 87103 provides that a public official has a financial
interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial
effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the official, a member of his or her
immediate family, or on any of the interests specified in Section 87103. Councilmember Rowse has
interests specified in Section 87103 as follows.

1. Any business entity in which Councilmember Rowse has a direct or indirect investment
worth $2,000 or more (Section 87103(a)) or a business entity in which he is a director, officer,
partner, trustee, employee, or holds any position of management. (Section 87103(d).)

Section 82005 defines a "business entity" as any organization or enterprise operated for
profit, including but not limited to a proprietorship, partnership, firm, business trust, joint venture,
syndicate, corporation or association. Paradise Café located at 702 Anacapa Street in Santa Barbara
is a business entity in which Councilmember Rowse has a financial interest.

2. Any real property in which Councilmember Rowse has a direct or indirect interest worth
$2,000 or more. (Section 87103(b).)

Section 82033 defines "interest in real property" to include "any leasehold, beneficial or
ownership interest or an option to acquire such an interest in real property located in the jurisdiction
owned directly, indirectly or beneficially by the public official, or other filer, or his or her
immediate family if the fair market value of the interest is two thousand dollars ($2,000) or more.
Interests in real property of an individual include a pro rata share of interests in real property of any

File No. A-16-089
Page No. 4

business entity or trust in which the individual or immediate family owns, directly, indirectly or
beneficially, a 10-percent interest or greater." Councilmember Rowse has a financial interest in his
leasehold for the Paradise Café location as this interest is worth $2,000 or more.

3. Source of income aggregating $500 or more in value provided or promised to or received
by Councilmember Rowse within 12 months prior to the time when the decision is made. (Section
87103(c).)

Councilmember Rowse's business is a source of income to him as well as an investment
interest because he presumably has received income of $500 or more in the past 12 months.

Under certain circumstances, a public official has an interest in the customers of the
official's business entity. If a public official owns a 10-percent or greater interest in the business,
customers who are sources of income to that business are also considered sources of income to the
public official. (Section 87103 (c).) If income derived from any particular client or customer
aggregates to $500 or more in the 12 months prior to the governmental decision, Councilmember
Rowse will have a financial interest in the client or customer as a source of income, unless the
exception for income from retail sales applies (See Section 87103.5.)

Foreseeability

The standard for foreseeability differs depending on whether or not an interest is explicitly
involved in the decision. A financial effect presumed to be reasonably
foreseable if the financial interest is a named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision
before the official or the official's agency. (Regulation 18701.)

Councilmember Rowse's business and leasehold are not the subject of the decision or a
named party in the decision. Additionally, you have not identified any customers to Councilmember
Rowse's business that may be the subject of or a named party in the decisions. Therefore,
Councilmember Rowse's financial interests are not explicitly involved in the decisions.

When a financial interest is not explicitly involved, a financial effect need not be likely to be
considered reasonably foreseeable. In general, if the financial effect can be recognized as a realistic
possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical, it is reasonably foreseeable. If the financial
result cannot be expected absent extraordinary circumstances not subject to the public official's
control, it is not reasonably foreseeable.

Regulation 18701 provides examples of situations where a financial effect is reasonably
foreseeable. Some of the factors found in Regulation 18701 are relevant to his business:

"(5) Whether the governmental decision will provide or deny an opportunity, or
create an advantage or disadvantage for one of the official's financial interests,
including whether the financial interest may be entitled to compete or be eligible
for a benefit resulting from the decision.

File No. A-16-089
Page No. 5

"(6) Whether the public official has the type of financial interest that would cause
a similarly situated person to weigh the advantages and disadvantages of the
governmental decision on his or her financial interest in formulating a position."

Currently, mobile food vending is prohibited in Santa Barbara. The proposed ordinance
would continue to prohibit mobile food vending in the area where Councilmember Rowse's
business is located, except for a daily two-hour period between 7:00 p.m. to 9:00 p.m. While mobile
food vending is a different style of food service compared to the Paradise Café restaurant owned by
Councilmember Rowse, the new ordinance would allow mobile food vending during a two-hour
window per day in an area where it was previously prohibited. This suggests that customers passing
by the food truck on the way to the Paradise Café may stop and choose to purchase food there
instead or vice versa. There is a realistic possibility that the ordinance could create an advantage or
disadvantage for Councilmember Rowse's business. Therefore, it is reasonably foreseeable that
there would be a financial effect upon Councilmember Rowse's financial interest in his business.

Next we must decide whether the reasonably foreseeable financial effect upon
Councilmember Rowse's business as an investment interest or source of income would be material.
We conclude that it would not be material. Regulation 18702.1 provides the materiality standards
for business entities as investment interests and when they are a source of income (Regulation
18702.3(a)(4).) Under Regulation 18702.1 a financial effect upon a business entity is material if the
governmental decision would contribute to a change in the value of the official's business entity. It
does not appear that the allowance of food truck vending for a two-hour window in the area where
the Paradise Café is located would create enough competition to contribute to a change in the value
of the Paradise Café because mobile food service is much different from the Paradise Café and the
vending would be limited to only two hours a day. Therefore, Councilmember Rowse is not
prohibited from participating in the decision of whether to adopt the draft ordinance.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:    Sukhi K. Brar
       Counsel, Legal Division

SKB:jgl

sections.question:
May Councilmember Rowse participate in a governmental decision involving a proposed 
ordinance that would allow mobile food vending in Santa Barbara in certain places and at certain 
times when he owns a business and leasehold in an area affected by the ordinance?
sections.conclusion:
Yes, Councilmember Rowse may participate in the governmental decision involving the 
proposed ordinance because there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of 
his financial interests.

QUESTION: May Councilmember Rowse participate in a governmental decision involving a proposed 
ordinance that would allow mobile food vending in Santa Barbara in certain places and at certain 
times when he owns a business and leasehold in an area affected by the ordinance?

CONCLUSION: Yes, Councilmember Rowse may participate in the governmental decision involving the 
proposed ordinance because there is no reasonably foreseeable material financial effect upon any of 
his financial interests.
